behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily deterministic backoff math/state progression. No real I/O, subprocess, or network is required to expose failures like wrong multiplier, overflow, or missing cap.
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure backoff calculator/state function with consecutive failure counts (e.g., 1..N), base delay, and max delay; assert delays follow 2^n growth and clamp at max.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: A pure mock can miss mismatches between HTTP response shape/parsing and retry classifier logic (e.g., status/header extraction). Wiring between transport response objects and retry policy must be exercised together.
behaviors.test_description: Use the real retry module plus real response parsing path with a fake in-process transport returning 429 and retry metadata; assert it is classified as rate-limit and the computed next-wait matches policy/header rules.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is orchestration across retry scheduler, request serialization, and transport invocation. Unit tests of pieces can miss that the original request is not reissued correctly.
behaviors.test_description: With fake timers and in-process fake transport: first call returns rate-limit, second call returns success. Assert transport is invoked twice with equivalent request payload and `call()` resolves with final success payload.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Type/shape of thrown error depends on retry-loop termination plus error mapping. Pure unit checks can miss generic error leakage from the transport layer.
behaviors.test_description: Drive the real retry wrapper with a fake transport that always returns rate-limit until retries are exhausted; assert rejection is `instanceof QuotaExhaustedError` (and not generic/transport error).

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Runaway loops usually emerge from interaction between retry counter, quota state, and timer scheduling. This requires modules wired together; no real external infra is necessary.
behaviors.test_description: Run end-to-end retry orchestration with fake timers and always-rate-limited responses; assert bounded attempt count, timer count, and eventual `QuotaExhaustedError` within expected virtual time.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: This is shared-state/concurrency behavior across multiple callers and library instances in-process. Unit tests on isolated functions can miss race and ownership bugs in quota coordination.
behaviors.test_description: Launch concurrent `call()` invocations against a shared client/quota manager with fake transport. Assert combined attempt accounting uses one shared bucket (global decrements/blocking), not per-caller independent quotas.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: FIFO dispatch depends on queue implementation plus scheduler interaction under contention. Mocking queue/scheduler separately can hide ordering regressions.
behaviors.test_description: Submit multiple calls while quota is exhausted, then advance fake time/release quota; assert transport send order matches submission order and completions map to corresponding requests.
